question: Mastercard reported fourth-quarter 2025 net revenue of $8.8 billion, up 18%, with purchase volume rising 9% on a local-currency basis.
Shopify reported fourth-quarter 2025 revenue growth of 31% and a 19% free cash flow margin, marking another quarter of double-digit free cash flow margin.
MercadoLibre delivered fourth-quarter 2025 net revenue and financial income of $8.8 billion, up 45% year over year.
Airbnb's fourth-quarter 2025 revenue rose 12%, while gross booking value increased 16% and nights and seats booked rose 10%.
Block reported fourth-quarter 2025 gross profit growth of 24% and raised its 2026 gross profit outlook.
PayPal grew 2025 revenue, transaction margin dollars, and earnings per share, but management said execution in branded checkout had not met expectations.
Procter & Gamble's fiscal second-quarter 2026 net sales rose 1%, but organic sales were flat and diluted earnings per share fell 5%.
3M's fourth-quarter 2025 adjusted EPS rose 9%, but GAAP EPS fell 20% as reported operating margin declined.
Booking Holdings lowered its annual revenue growth forecast after Middle East travel disruptions reduced room night growth and weighed on demand.
Cisco reported second-quarter fiscal 2026 revenue of $15.3 billion and raised its full-year outlook as AI infrastructure demand strengthened.
$MA net revenue up 18% with purchase volume still growing makes the payments story look very solid.
$SHOP growing revenue 31% and still posting a 19% free cash flow margin is a strong combination.
$MELI putting up 45% revenue growth again shows Latin American commerce and fintech demand are still running hot.
$ABNB finally reaccelerating bookings and revenue in Q4 makes the travel platform look healthier.
$XYZ gross profit growth jumping 24% and guidance moving higher makes the Block turnaround look real.
$PYPL numbers improved for 2025, but management admitting branded checkout execution problems keeps me cautious.
$PG still sells a lot, but flat organic sales and lower EPS make the quarter feel stuck.
$MMM adjusted results look better, but GAAP EPS dropping 20% makes the headline quarter hard to love.
$BKNG cutting its revenue growth outlook because travel demand is getting hit is not what holders wanted.
$CSCO taking in big AI infrastructure demand and lifting the outlook makes old-school networking look relevant again.
Fourth-quarter net revenue increased 18%, supported by higher purchase volume and continued cross-border payment activity.
We delivered 31% revenue growth in the fourth quarter while maintaining a 19% free cash flow margin.
Net revenue and financial income increased 45% year over year, reflecting continued strength across commerce and fintech.
Revenue, gross booking value, and nights and seats booked all grew at double-digit rates in the fourth quarter.
Gross profit grew 24% in the fourth quarter, and we raised our 2026 gross profit and adjusted operating income outlook.
Revenue and earnings improved during 2025, although branded checkout execution remains below the level we need.
Net sales increased slightly, but organic sales were unchanged and earnings per share declined because of restructuring charges.
Adjusted earnings improved in the quarter, while reported earnings declined due to weaker GAAP operating margin.
Travel demand remains disrupted in key regions, requiring us to reduce our annual revenue growth expectations.
Second-quarter revenue reached $15.3 billion, and AI infrastructure orders supported a higher full-year outlook.
Sasol received certification for sustainable aviation fuel production, allowing it to begin working toward exports to the European Union.
LyondellBasell signed European wind-power purchase agreements to increase renewable electricity use across its operations.
Air bp signed a multi-year sustainable aviation fuel supply contract with Airbus across Germany and Spain.
Mastercard reported that revenue grew in 2025 while emissions declined for a third consecutive year.
TotalEnergies agreed to supply 800 gigawatt-hours of renewable electricity to a paper manufacturer in France over ten years.
An investor climate group relaunched with weaker pledges after climate-focused initiatives lost support from major investors.
Environmental groups in the Western Balkans urged governments to prioritize renewable power over new gas projects to avoid locking in fossil-fuel dependence.
U.S. renewable-energy firms faced pressure from policy shifts that accelerated consolidation and asset sales in the solar and wind sector.
Google agreed to buy biochar carbon removal credits from an Indian agricultural-waste project, expanding corporate support for carbon removal.
Portugal announced a large resilience investment plan to strengthen infrastructure after storms and a major blackout.
Norway cut its 2026 mainland economic growth forecast to 1.8% from 2.1%, signaling a weaker activity outlook.
Sweden kept its policy rate unchanged at 1.75% and projected stronger GDP growth of about 3.0% in 2026 after 1.0% growth in 2025.
Poland's economy was expected to grow more than 3% in 2026, but the IMF warned that the country still needed to curb rising public debt.
Portugal's 2026 budget forecast stronger growth, a small budget surplus, and a lower debt-to-GDP ratio.
The IMF cut Turkey's 2026 growth forecast from 4.2% to 3.4% because of weaker momentum and higher energy prices.
Turkey's annual inflation fell to 30.87% in March and came in below market forecasts, although price growth remained very high.
Egypt's economic growth outlook was trimmed as higher energy prices and regional conflict threatened tourism, Suez Canal revenue, and remittances.
The World Bank projected a 24% surge in energy prices in 2026, increasing inflation pressure on developing economies.
Sweden issued an early warning about a possible jet fuel shortage after Middle East conflict disrupted global oil supply routes.
Portugal's economy rebounded with 0.6% quarterly growth in the second quarter of 2025 after contracting in the previous quarter.
